topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with a low, annoying buzz, and I kept tapping my pen against the worn wooden table. Liam was laughing, a bright, easy sound that always used to mean something to me, but now it just felt like background noise. He leaned back, recounting some anecdote, and I watched the familiar spark in his eyes—the one I used to recognize instantly—and felt a tight knot tighten in my chest. "Seriously, Liam, that was a ridiculous thing to say," I cut in, my voice sharper than intended. He glanced over, a flicker of surprise crossing his face, quickly smoothed over by a shrug. "Yeah, well, you know how it is." The casual dismissal stung, a dull, persistent ache behind my ribs. I picked up my mug, the ceramic suddenly feeling too hot in my hands, and turned my attention to the condensation tracing paths down the glass. It was just… pointless.

[story 2]

I stood by the lockers, meticulously re-folding my backpack, the movement jerky and overly precise. Every time Liam walked past, I braced myself, waiting for the usual greeting, the shared inside joke that would bridge the gap between us. Nothing. He bumped shoulders with me, a brief, accidental contact, and he offered a polite, distant nod. "Hey," he said, his tone flat, devoid of the warmth I craved. It was like watching a poorly dubbed movie; the words were there, but the feeling was missing entirely. I clenched my jaw, feeling the tension pool in my neck. I adjusted the strap of my bag again, deliberately making the sound of the zipper loud and abrasive. Why did it have to feel like such a monumental effort just to acknowledge my existence?

[story 3]

"So, remember that summer trip?" I asked, trying to keep the edge out of my voice, hoping for some shared memory to anchor us. Liam looked up from his phone, a slight furrow in his brow, as if accessing a distant file. He paused, then shook his head slowly. "I don't think so. That sounds like something you might have imagined." The air seemed to thicken around me. I felt a sudden, unwelcome heat rise up my cheeks. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but I held my breath instead, forcing my hands to remain still at my sides. I let out a slow, controlled exhale, the sound barely audible, and turned away, walking toward the exit with a stiff, unnatural gait, determined not to let the frustration show in the way I knew it would.
